message.content:
User Execution: Malicious File involves adversaries tricking users into opening malicious files to gain code execution. This technique often follows spearphishing attachments and employs various file types (.doc, .pdf, .xls, .exe, etc.). Adversaries use social engineering, masquerading, and obfuscation to increase the likelihood of execution, such as familiar naming conventions or password-protected files with instructions. This method can occur at different intrusion phases, including placing files in shared directories or desktops.

The impact includes unauthorized code execution, leading to potential data breaches, malware installation, and further system compromise. Common exploitation methods include spearphishing emails with malicious attachments, luring users to enable macros, and disguising malware as legitimate software or documents.

Mitigation strategies include user training on recognizing phishing attempts, implementing email filtering to block malicious attachments, disabling macros by default, and using endpoint protection to detect and block malicious file execution. Regular software updates and patches can also reduce vulnerabilities that adversaries might exploit.
